explanation: The input specification describes that, in PCV mode during the expiration phase, if no spontaneous breath is detected such that 'dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PCV' is false, and during this expiration time 'expiratoryPauseButton' is set, then the system must ensure that 'expiratoryPause' is triggered (i.e., the Expiratory Pause starts). In LTL, we express this as: Always (if in PCV mode and in expiratory phase and not detecting a spontaneous breath, and the expiratory pause button is pressed, then eventually (within that expiration) an expiratory pause will start). Since we do not have explicit time intervals as atoms, we indicate it with implication leading to the eventuality of 'expiratoryPause'.

The input natural language describes a requirement in PCV mode: if the controller is in the expiration phase, and a spontaneous breath is not detected (i.e., drop(PAW) > ITS_PCV is false), and the Expiratory Pause has been set by the GUI (expiratoryPauseButton), then within the expiration time, an Expiratory Pause shall start (expiratoryPause). In LTL, this can be captured by stating: whenever the controller is in expiratoryPhase AND NOT dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PCV (i.e., spontaneous breath not detected) AND expiratoryPauseButton is pressed, it MUST eventually be the case (within that expiratoryPhase segment, but since we can't use clocks or explicit interval constraints, we capture the response within the current expiratoryPhase) that expiratoryPause becomes true, as long as expiratoryPhase is still active. This is encoded by: globally, if we are in expiratory phase, exp pause button set, and no drop(PAW) > ITS_PCV, then until expiratory phase ends, if the precondition holds, eventually expiratoryPause should hold. (Note: With no interval operators, this is the closest safe LTL translation.)

The goal is to formalize: Whenever the controller is in PCV mode, is in the expiratory phase, does NOT detect a spontaneous breath ('dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PCV' is false) during expiration, and the Expiratory Pause button is set, then the Expiratory Pause must eventually start before expiration ends.

To capture this, we use LTL to express: globally, if at any moment all these conditions hold (PCVMode, expiratoryPhase, !dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PCV, expiratoryPauseButton), then eventually, before expiration ends ('expiratoryPhase' becomes false), 'expiratoryPause' must hold. Since release/strong until over phases is awkward without explicit phase-end indicators as triggers, we simply require: whenever in PCVMode, in the expiratoryPhase, no spontaneous breath, and button pressed, then eventually 'expiratoryPause' occurs.

A subtlety is that in real implementation, the requirement would bound the response within the expiration phase, but in basic LTL, we capture the core causal safety property: 'if all enabling conditions then eventually expiratoryPause.'

The natural language describes a requirement in PCV Mode for the controller when in the expiration phase: if no spontaneous breath is detected (i.e., a drop in airway pressure above ITS_PCV is NOT detected), and within the expiration phase time, if the Expiratory Pause is requested via the GUI, then an Expiratory Pause shall begin. Translated into LTL, this means: globally, during expiration phase, if (dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PCV is NOT true at any moment in expiration phase), and if during this phase the expiratoryPauseButton is pressed, then eventually within expiration phase, expiratoryPause must occur. The translation focuses on the relationships and temporal ordering: (1) in expiration phase, (2) absence of dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PCV (which means no spontaneous breath detected), (3) request for expiratoryPauseButton, and (4) guarantee that expiratoryPause initiates. This LTL property encodes this by conditioning expPause request and absence of spontaneous breath detection (negated dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PCV) during expiration phase to always eventually cause expiratoryPause.
output_LTL: G( PCVMode & expiratoryPhase & !dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PCV & expiratoryPauseButton -> F (expiratoryPause & expiratoryPhase) )

explanation: The natural language statement describes a scenario in PCV mode during the expiration phase. If a spontaneous breath (dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PCV) is NOT detected during expiration, and the GUI sets expiratoryPauseButton, then an Expiratory Pause (expiratoryPause) must start before expiration ends (i.e., within the expiration time). LTL does not allow explicit time-bounded operators or direct referencing of value intervals, so we must express the condition as: whenever in the expiratoryPhase, if we have not detected dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PCV and expiratoryPauseButton is pressed, then eventually (before expiration ends) expiratoryPause must occur. This is approximated by insisting that, globally, whenever we enter expirationPhase, if expiratoryPauseButton is raised and no dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PCV occurs (i.e., (!dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PCV) holds throughout the expiratory phase), we require that until expiratoryPhase ends (expiratoryPhaseEnd), if expiratoryPause hasn't occurred yet, it must eventually occur during that period. This is encoded by using U (until) and G (globally). Note: We avoid numeric comparisons and ensure atomic propositions only are used.
output_LTL: G( expiratoryPhase -> ( (( expiratoryPauseButton & G( expiratoryPhase -> !dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PCV ) ) -> ( (!expiratoryPause) U (expiratoryPause | expiratoryPhaseEnd) ) ) ) )

explanation:
The natural language states that: if the controller is in the expiration phase, and no spontaneous breath is detected (i.e., 'drop(PAW) > ITS_PCV' is false), within the expiration time, if the Expiratory Pause is set by the GUI ('expiratoryPauseButton'), then an Expiratory Pause ('expiratoryPause') should start.

Translating to LTL: 'expiratoryPhase' indicates we are in expiration. '!dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PCV' captures that drop(PAW) > ITS_PCV is false. 'expiratoryPauseButton' is the user's request for an Expiratory Pause. The property must ensure that whenever all these hold (simultaneously), then eventually within the same expiration, 'expiratoryPause' will hold (i.e., the Expiratory Pause starts).

To express that if expiration is ongoing, no spontaneous breath detected, and the pause is requested, then 'expiratoryPause' must occur at least once in the current expiration period, we use:
Globally: if there is a point where 'expiratoryPhase' & '!dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PCV' & 'expiratoryPauseButton' is true, then 'expiratoryPause' holds at some point before the expiration ends.
This is represented as: G((expiratoryPhase & !dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PCV & expiratoryPauseButton) -> (expiratoryPhase U (expiratoryPause | !expiratoryPhase)))

- The antecedent checks: all the conditions are present now.
- The consequent (expiratoryPhase U (expiratoryPause | !expiratoryPhase)) expresses: as long as expiration is ongoing, eventually (before the expiratory phase ends), either the Expiratory Pause starts, or the phase ends.

If 'expiratoryPause' never occurs before expiration ends, this property is violated. This formulation restricts the reaction to the current expiratory phase.

This is superior to alternatives like a simple implication to 'F expiratoryPause' because that could allow the pause to start after expiration ends, which is not what is required.

This property formalizes the requirement that whenever the expiratory phase is active (expiratoryPhase), and a spontaneous breath has NOT been detected (i.e., the condition dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PCV is FALSE, so we use !dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PCV), and the expiratoryPauseButton is set (Expiratory Pause requested by GUI) during expiration, then eventually during expiration, the expiratoryPause must be initiated (expiratoryPause). Since there is no direct proposition for 'within expiration time' or for the interval, we express this causality within the scope of the expiratory phase: globally, whenever all the conditions hold, it must be that eventually, while still in expiration, the expiratory pause will occur. The property avoids extra numerical expressions and makes the relationship between the phase, button, and detection explicit. Previous attempts that do not scope the 'eventually' to the expiratoryPhase (or fail to require all three conditions at the same time as the cause) may either over- or under-constrain the temporal relationship between these events. By using the 'G' for globally and '->' for implication, and scoping the eventual response to occur while expiry phase still holds, this property best captures the intended requirement.

The property requires that, in PCV mode, whenever the system is in the expiratory phase and does NOT detect a spontaneous breath (i.e., the condition drop(PAW) > ITS_PCV is false, so !dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PCV), AND during the expiratory phase, if the Expiratory Pause start is set via the GUI (expiratoryPauseButton), then an Expiratory Pause (expiratoryPause) must follow. The ordering implied is: 'if at some point during the expiratory phase (under these conditions) the Expiratory Pause is requested, then an Expiratory Pause must happen.'

We encode this with a global (G) formula over time: when in PCVMode AND expiratoryPhase AND NOT dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PCV, whenever expiratoryPauseButton becomes true, eventually expiratoryPause is set (must respond within the expiration time, but no explicit expiration timer atomic proposition is available; this will be as close as possible with available APs).

The given natural language requirement specifies a conditional action during the expiration phase in PCV mode: If, while in the expiration phase, no spontaneous breath (as indicated by dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PCV being false) is detected within the expiration time, and the expiratoryPauseButton (expiratory pause requested by the GUI) is activated, then an expiratoryPause must begin.

We capture this in LTL by expressing: Whenever the system enters the expiratory phase (expiratoryPhaseStart), if at some point the expiratoryPauseButton is pressed, and throughout the expiratory phase dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PCV does not occur (i.e., the patient does not initiate a breath), then eventually within the phase, expiratoryPause must be asserted. Since we do not have explicit time constraints (expiration time is not represented by a proposition), we rely on the activation within the phase.

The property uses G to specify that this holds globally, and U to ensure the 'until' structure within each expiratory phase segment. The implication is conditioned on the atomic propositions we have. This avoids numerical comparisons and stays true to the available propositions.

LTL: Globally, whenever expiratoryPhase is active, if expiratoryPauseButton is pressed and (while !)dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PCV) holds, then eventually during the phase expiratoryPause happens.

The requirement states: If the controller is in expiration phase, and does NOT detect a spontaneous breath (i.e., dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PCV is FALSE), and within the expiration time (so this applies during the expiratory phase), if the Expiratory Pause start is set by the GUI (expiratoryPauseButton), an expiratory pause shall start (expiratoryPause). In LTL: Whenever we are in the expiratory phase, and (dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PCV is not true) and expiratoryPauseButton is true within the expiration window, then expiratoryPause shall occur. Because LTL cannot use explicit time intervals or numerical relations, we abstract 'within expiration time' as a condition that needs to be globally true during the expiratoryPhase. The most direct encoding is: Globally, whenever we are in expiratoryPhase AND dropPAW_greaterThan_ITS_PCV is false AND expiratoryPauseButton occurs, then eventually expiratoryPause must become true (since we can't specify time bounds, it is 'eventually' after the combined preconditions).
